planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; cempedak is a Southeast Asian Artocarpus fruit tree closely related to jackfruit but considered by many connoisseurs to produce richer, sweeter, more aromatic fruits — with the flesh also frequently battered and fried in Malaysia; the tree tolerates more shade than jackfruit, making it useful in multi-story food forests.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; cempedak tolerates partial shade better than jackfruit — useful in the middle canopy of tropical food forests. Artocarpus integer (cempedak or champedak) is a Southeast Asian fruit tree in the breadfruit family (Moraceae) — a close relative of jackfruit (A. heterophyllus) native to the Malay Peninsula, Borneo, and Sumatra. Like jackfruit, cempedak produces large composite fruits on the trunk and branches through cauliflory, but the fruits are smaller than jackfruit (typically 6–15 inches long, 4–6 inches diameter), cylindrical, and covered in small, soft, hexagonal-patterned protrusions with a yellow to orange-green exterior. The interior fruit segments are rich, sweet, aromatic, and exceptionally good — widely considered by Malaysian and Indonesian fruit lovers to be superior in flavor to jackfruit, with a denser, creamier, more strongly tropical-fruit character combining mango, banana, and custard apple notes. Cempedak is particularly popular in Malaysia fried: the individual fruit segments are battered and deep-fried as a street food (exactly like a tropical doughnut), which is a major culinary tradition. The seeds are edible when roasted or boiled. Cempedak is more shade-tolerant than jackfruit, making it useful as a middle-canopy tree in multi-story agroforestry systems. Requires genuinely tropical conditions (zone 11–12) without frost.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Artocarpus integer
Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute (MARDI)
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade; monkey jack is a medium-sized Artocarpus species from lowland Southeast Asian forests producing round cauliflorous composite fruits with orange-yellow sweet flesh — smaller and more manageable than jackfruit, and more shade-tolerant.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade; monkey jack is more shade-tolerant than jackfruit and suited to multi-story food forest understory planting. Artocarpus rigidus (monkey jack, pingan, or terap hutan) is a tropical Artocarpus species from the lowland dipterocarp forests of Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines — one of several dozen Artocarpus species in the breadfruit and jackfruit genus, most of which produce edible composite fruits through cauliflory (fruiting on the trunk and main branches). Monkey jack produces round to oval, 2–4 inch composite fruits with a spiny-bumpy, green exterior and orange-yellow, sweet, somewhat sticky flesh surrounding multiple seeds. The flavor is sweet, fruity, and pleasant when fully ripe, though less refined in flavor than the closely related cempedak (A. integer) or jackfruit (A. heterophyllus). It is primarily a forest fruit eaten opportunistically in Malaysia and Indonesia rather than a major cultivated crop, and is notable mainly to tropical fruit collectors and ethnobotanists documenting the edible Artocarpus species of Borneo. As a garden tree, monkey jack is smaller and more manageable than jackfruit, with good shade tolerance making it useful in food forest designs. Restricted to zone 11–12 humid tropics.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Artocarpus species

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, loamy soil in full sun; black cumin (kala jeera or shahi jeera) is the genuine Kashmiri and Persian 'royal cumin' — an Apiaceae plant from Central Asian mountain meadows with dramatically different flavor from common cumin, black seed, and nigella, used as the defining spice in Kashmiri rice dishes, korma, and Persian breads.

directions: Direct sow in early spring (4–6 weeks before last frost) in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; thin to 6 inches; harvest seed heads when fully mature and dry. Bunium persicum (black cumin, kala jeera, or shahi jeera — literally 'royal cumin' in Urdu) is a wild carrot family (Apiaceae) plant from the mountain steppes and alpine meadows of Central Asia — particularly Kashmir, Iran, Afghanistan, and the Hindu Kush range — and represents the genuine botanical source of the highly aromatic spice sold in Indian specialty markets as 'kala jeera' or 'shahi jeera.' It is frequently confused with two other spices sharing the common name 'black cumin': Nigella sativa (black seed or black caraway, an entirely different plant) and regular cumin (Cuminum cyminum). Bunium persicum has a uniquely complex, intense, earthy-herbal-thyme-like aroma with bitter-sweet warmth — dramatically different in character from common cumin, more aromatic and complex, with additional notes reminiscent of dried thyme and warm spice. In Kashmiri cooking, kala jeera is the defining spice of pulao rice (the famous Kashmiri mutton pulao), yakhni lamb broth, and korma dishes. In Persian cuisine, it flavors traditional breads, rice dishes, and pickles. The plant grows as a delicate, feathery-leaved annual resembling miniature Queen Anne's lace, producing small umbel flowers followed by the narrow, ridged, black-brown aromatic seeds.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Spices and Herbs
Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Medicinal and Aromatic Plants
American Botanical Council — Ethnobotany of Kashmiri Spices

planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or gravelly soil in full sun; za'atar savory is a drought-tolerant subshrubby perennial from the Eastern Mediterranean dry hillsides — one of the specific plants contributing to za'atar spice blends, with a warm, thyme-oregano-savory flavor — requiring excellent drainage above all else.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, dry, rocky or gravelly, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; drought-tolerant once established; avoid overwatering — this plant demands dry conditions. Satureja thymbra (za'atar savory, pink savory, or thyme-leaved savory) is a compact, woody-based perennial from the dry limestone hillsides, macchia scrub, and garrigue of the Eastern Mediterranean — native to Greece, Turkey, Cyprus, Lebanon, and Israel. It is one of several specific plants that go into the za'atar herb blend of the Middle East and Eastern Mediterranean — alongside Origanum syriacum, Thymbra spicata, and Micromeria fruticosa — and is also used independently as a culinary and medicinal herb. The flavor of S. thymbra is warm, complex, and intense: more resinous and thyme-like than common summer savory (S. hortensis), with oregano notes and a subtle bitterness that adds depth to za'atar blends, grilled meats, and herb breads. The plant forms a neat, mounding, evergreen (in mild winters) subshrub with small, deep-green, aromatic leaves and pink to light-purple flowers that attract bees abundantly in early summer. It performs well in Mediterranean-climate gardens (zone 8–10) with excellent drainage and lean soil — exactly the conditions found on Greek island hillsides.
sources: Herb Society of America — Za'atar and Related Herbs
Royal Botanic Garden Kew — Plants of the World Online
University of California Cooperative Extension — Mediterranean Culinary Herbs

planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or gravelly soil in full sun; white micromeria (zuta levanit in Hebrew, meaning 'white za'atar') is an Eastern Mediterranean native subshrub with white-hairy oval leaves and a gentle thyme-mint tea flavor — used in Israel and the Levant both as a traditional herbal tea and as a component of za'atar spice blends.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, dry, rocky or gravelly, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; drought-tolerant once established; trim lightly after flowering to maintain compact form. Micromeria fruticosa (white micromeria, white za'atar, or zuta levanit) is a small aromatic subshrub native to the dry rocky hillsides, terra rossa soils, and limestone outcrops of the Eastern Mediterranean — particularly Israel, Jordan, Lebanon, and the Palestinian territories. The plant has distinctive small, oval, white-hairy (tomentose) leaves with a soft, felted texture, giving the plant an overall white-gray appearance; hence 'white' za'atar or zuta levanit. The fragrance and flavor are unique: a gentle, warm, herbal-thyme character with a subtle mint and tea-like undertone — softer and more delicate than either thyme or savory. In Israel and the Levant, M. fruticosa is one of the most beloved traditional herbal teas, prepared by infusing fresh or dried leaves in hot water; it is believed to be calming, digestive, and to relieve cold symptoms. It is also one of the plants historically used in za'atar spice blends in the Levant, where different communities have distinct formulations using various combinations of Origanum syriacum, Thymbra spicata, Satureja thymbra, and M. fruticosa. A delightful low-growing groundcover for sunny, dry, Mediterranean-climate gardens.
sources: Herb Society of America — Za'atar Herb Complex
University of Haifa — Eastern Mediterranean Ethnobotany
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, gravelly or sandy soil in full sun; conehead thyme (Thymbra capitata, sometimes sold as Thymus capitatus) is a strong-flavored Mediterranean subshrub from dry hillsides of Spain, Portugal, and the Eastern Mediterranean — sold commercially in some markets as 'Spanish oregano' due to its intense oregano-thyme flavor, and one of the traditional za'atar herbs of the region.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, dry, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; extremely drought-tolerant; the most flavorful and intense of all thyme-related herbs. Thymbra capitata (conehead thyme, also known as Coridothymus capitatus or sold as Thymus capitatus) is a compact, thorny-based, perennial aromatic subshrub from the dry Mediterranean hillsides of Spain, Portugal, the Balearic Islands, and extending east through the Mediterranean basin. It is one of the most intensely aromatic herbs in the Mediterranean flora — the small, crowded, stiff leaves and pink flower heads (arranged in the characteristic rounded 'cone' clusters that give the plant its name) contain very high concentrations of carvacrol and thymol, the same aromatic compounds found in both thyme and oregano, but at higher concentrations than either cultivated thyme or Greek oregano. The flavor is powerfully savory, hot, and intensely aromatic — making it highly valued as a culinary herb (used as a substitute for oregano in Spanish cooking), as one of the traditional components of Mediterranean za'atar blends, and as a source of essential oil. In Israel and the Levant, dried conehead thyme is an important za'atar component. The plant requires perfectly drained, lean, alkaline, sun-baked conditions — overwatering or clay soil will kill it. In zone 8–10 Mediterranean-climate gardens, it is long-lived, decorative, and worth growing as a specimen culinary herb.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Mediterranean Culinary Herbs
Royal Botanic Garden Kew — Plants of the World Online
Herb Society of America — Thyme and Relatives
